questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A test that consistently produces the same results on repeated administrations demonstrates:

choices.text: Validity
choices.explanation: Validity measures whether a test measures what it is intended to measure — consistency of results across administrations is reliability.

choices.text: Standardization
choices.explanation: Standardization ensures all test-takers experience the same conditions — it is a prerequisite for reliability but not the same concept.

choices.text: Reliability
choices.explanation: Reliability is the consistency of a test — if it produces the same results on repeated administrations (test-retest reliability) or between different raters (inter-rater reliability), the test is reliable.

choices.text: Predictive validity
choices.explanation: Predictive validity is how well a test predicts future performance — while related to reliability, this specifically describes consistency, not prediction.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Intelligence tests that have the same content and are scored the same way for all test-takers have been:

choices.text: Validated
choices.explanation: Validation determines whether a test measures what it claims to measure — using the same content and scoring for all is standardization.

choices.text: Standardized
choices.explanation: Standardization involves using uniform procedures for administration and scoring so that all test-takers are measured under the same conditions, making their scores comparable.

choices.text: Normed
choices.explanation: Norming establishes comparison scores from a representative sample — standardization refers to consistent administration and scoring procedures.

choices.text: Adapted
choices.explanation: Adapting tests involves modifying content for different populations — standardization refers to uniform application to all test-takers.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A test can be reliable but not valid. Which example best illustrates this?

choices.text: A scale that consistently reads 5 pounds too heavy
choices.explanation: A scale consistently 5 pounds too heavy is reliable (same wrong reading every time) but not valid (doesn't accurately measure true weight) — demonstrating that reliability without validity is possible.

choices.text: An IQ test that predicts academic performance well
choices.explanation: A test that predicts academic performance has predictive validity — and presumably reliability too.

choices.text: A ruler that gives different measurements each time
choices.explanation: Different measurements each time indicates neither reliability nor validity.

choices.text: A personality test normed on a diverse population
choices.explanation: Normative data improves a test's usefulness — but the relationship between reliability and validity is shown by the consistently-wrong scale example.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Alfred Binet developed his intelligence test primarily to:

choices.text: Rank military recruits for officer positions
choices.explanation: Military intelligence testing (Army Alpha/Beta) was developed during WWI — Binet developed his test for the French school system.

choices.text: Identify French schoolchildren who needed additional academic help
choices.explanation: Binet created the first practical intelligence test in 1905 to identify children in Paris schools who needed special educational assistance — the test was designed for educational placement, not ranking.

choices.text: Measure innate, fixed intelligence for research purposes
choices.explanation: Binet explicitly believed intelligence was not fixed and that education could improve performance — he opposed the idea that his test measured innate, unchangeable ability.

choices.text: Predict criminal behavior in juvenile offenders
choices.explanation: Binet's test was for educational purposes, not criminology — though some early misapplications of intelligence testing did involve such uses.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Intelligence scores in a large, representative population tend to form which distribution?

choices.text: A skewed distribution with most scores in the upper range
choices.explanation: Intelligence test scores are deliberately designed to produce a normal distribution centered around 100 — not a skewed distribution.

choices.text: A bimodal distribution with peaks at high and low scores
choices.explanation: A bimodal distribution would indicate two distinct groups — intelligence scores form a normal (bell-curve) distribution in the general population.

choices.text: A normal (bell-curve) distribution centered at 100
choices.explanation: IQ scores are designed through standardization to produce a normal distribution with a mean of 100 and standard deviation of 15 — about 68% of the population scores between 85 and 115.

choices.text: A uniform distribution with equal numbers at all score levels
choices.explanation: Intelligence scores cluster around the mean (100) with fewer scores at the extremes — a normal distribution, not a uniform one.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Charles Spearman's concept of 'g' refers to:

choices.text: A specific type of visual-spatial intelligence identified through factor analysis
choices.explanation: Spearman's 'g' is a general factor that underlies all cognitive abilities — not a specific visual-spatial ability.

choices.text: A general intelligence factor that underlies performance on all cognitive tasks
choices.explanation: Through factor analysis, Spearman identified 'g' — a general intelligence factor that accounts for the positive correlations between diverse cognitive tests — suggesting a common underlying ability.

choices.text: The average intelligence quotient across all people globally
choices.explanation: 'g' is not an average score — it is a statistical factor derived from correlations between different cognitive tests.

choices.text: Gardner's theory of multiple independent intelligences
choices.explanation: Gardner proposed multiple independent intelligences — the opposite view from Spearman's single 'g' factor.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Howard Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences challenged traditional IQ tests by arguing that:

choices.text: Intelligence is a single, fixed trait that cannot be changed
choices.explanation: Gardner argued the opposite — intelligence is not a single fixed trait but multiple relatively independent abilities.

choices.text: There are multiple distinct intelligences that IQ tests fail to fully capture
choices.explanation: Gardner proposed 8+ types of intelligence (linguistic, logical-mathematical, spatial, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalist) — arguing that traditional IQ tests measure only a narrow range of abilities.

choices.text: Cultural factors completely determine intellectual performance
choices.explanation: Gardner proposed biologically based, distinct intelligences — not culturally determined performance.

choices.text: Intelligence only exists as the sum of specific narrow abilities
choices.explanation: Gardner proposed distinct, relatively independent intelligences — not simply a sum of narrow abilities, which is closer to the 's' factor concept.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Robert Sternberg's triarchic theory proposes three types of intelligence. Which of the following is NOT one of them?

choices.text: Analytical intelligence
choices.explanation: Analytical intelligence (academic, problem-solving) is one of Sternberg's three types — it is included in the triarchic theory.

choices.text: Creative intelligence
choices.explanation: Creative intelligence (generating novel ideas, dealing with new situations) is one of Sternberg's three types.

choices.text: Emotional intelligence
choices.explanation: Emotional intelligence was proposed by Salovey and Mayer (and popularized by Goleman) — it is NOT part of Sternberg's triarchic theory, which includes analytical, creative, and practical intelligence.

choices.text: Practical intelligence
choices.explanation: Practical intelligence (applying knowledge to real-world situations, 'street smarts') is one of Sternberg's three types.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Flynn effect refers to:

choices.text: The tendency for IQ scores to decline with age in adulthood
choices.explanation: The Flynn effect describes a population-level rise in IQ scores over generations — not a decline within individuals.

choices.text: The generational rise in average IQ scores observed over the 20th century
choices.explanation: The Flynn effect (named after James Flynn) documents that average IQ scores have risen substantially in many countries over decades — suggesting environmental factors (nutrition, education, complex environments) can raise IQ.

choices.text: The lower average IQ scores of minority groups compared to majority groups
choices.explanation: This describes group score differences — the Flynn effect refers to the rise in scores over generations within populations.

choices.text: The genetic ceiling on intelligence development
choices.explanation: The Flynn effect actually argues against a genetic ceiling — rising scores across generations demonstrate environmental influences on IQ.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Stereotype threat, as described by Claude Steele, refers to:

choices.text: The tendency for stereotypes to accurately predict group performance on tests
choices.explanation: Stereotype threat explains performance gaps as situational, not as accurate reflections of ability — it is specifically about how awareness of stereotypes harms performance.

choices.text: Concern that one will confirm a negative stereotype about one's group, which impairs performance
choices.explanation: Stereotype threat is the situational predicament where people in stigmatized groups fear confirming a negative stereotype — this anxiety consumes cognitive resources and impairs performance, contributing to test score gaps.

choices.text: The use of stereotypes by test designers to create biased questions
choices.explanation: Test bias refers to questions that unfairly advantage certain groups — stereotype threat is about the psychological impact on test-takers, not question design.

choices.text: The inability of minority group members to score well due to genetic limitations
choices.explanation: This is a racist interpretation refuted by Steele's research — stereotype threat demonstrates that score gaps can be situationally reduced by removing threatening cues, showing they reflect situation, not genetic limitation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Fluid intelligence, as described by Cattell, is best defined as:

choices.text: Accumulated knowledge and verbal skills built through experience
choices.explanation: Accumulated knowledge and verbal skills describe crystallized intelligence — fluid intelligence involves real-time reasoning ability.

choices.text: The ability to reason speedily and think flexibly to solve novel problems
choices.explanation: Fluid intelligence is the ability to reason and solve novel problems in the moment — independent of specific knowledge or experience. It peaks in early adulthood and declines with age.

choices.text: Emotional awareness and social understanding
choices.explanation: Emotional awareness and social understanding describe emotional intelligence — not fluid intelligence.

choices.text: The number of items one can hold in working memory simultaneously
choices.explanation: Working memory capacity is one component of fluid intelligence, but fluid intelligence is the broader ability to reason about novel problems.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Tests designed to predict future performance rather than measure current achievement are called:

choices.text: Achievement tests
choices.explanation: Achievement tests measure what a person has already learned in a specific subject area — aptitude tests predict future performance potential.

choices.text: Aptitude tests
choices.explanation: Aptitude tests are designed to predict future performance by measuring potential and specific abilities — the SAT was originally called the Scholastic Aptitude Test.

choices.text: Projective tests
choices.explanation: Projective tests (like the Rorschach) use ambiguous stimuli to assess personality — they are not designed to predict academic performance.

choices.text: Personality inventories
choices.explanation: Personality inventories assess stable personality traits — not designed to predict future academic or job performance.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research consistently shows that the heritability of intelligence is approximately 50% in children. This means that:

choices.text: Exactly half of a person's intelligence is genetic and half is environmental
choices.explanation: Heritability is a population statistic, not an individual partition — it tells us what proportion of variance across individuals is attributable to genetic differences, not how much of any individual's IQ is 'from genes.'

choices.text: About 50% of the variation in intelligence scores across individuals in the population is attributable to genetic differences
choices.explanation: Heritability is a population-level statistic indicating what proportion of the observed variation in a trait (like IQ) across individuals is due to genetic differences — not a statement about any one person's intelligence.

choices.text: Intelligence cannot be changed by environmental interventions
choices.explanation: High heritability does not mean immutability — IQ responds to environmental interventions (education, nutrition) even when heritability is high, as shown by the Flynn effect.

choices.text: Identical twins will always have exactly the same IQ
choices.explanation: Identical twins share genes but grow up in somewhat different environments — heritability is about population-level variance, not perfect within-pair similarity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A test that measures knowledge of American cultural content would likely have which problem when administered internationally?

choices.text: Low reliability across administrations
choices.explanation: Cultural content doesn't affect reliability (consistency) — the problem is that it disadvantages test-takers from different cultural backgrounds.

choices.text: Cultural bias — unfairly disadvantaging non-American test-takers
choices.explanation: A test with American cultural content would be culturally biased — it would measure cultural familiarity rather than the intended construct (like intelligence), systematically disadvantaging non-American test-takers.

choices.text: Excessive difficulty making scores negatively skewed
choices.explanation: Difficulty level is a separate issue from cultural bias — the test would show cultural bias because it measures cultural knowledge rather than underlying ability.

choices.text: Low predictive validity for American students
choices.explanation: Cultural content would likely maintain predictive validity for American students — the problem is bias against non-American test-takers.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: What is the average IQ score in the general population, by design?

choices.explanation: 85 is one standard deviation below average — the mean IQ score is defined as 100.
IQ tests are standardized so that the average score is 100 — individual scores are expressed relative to how far they deviate from this average, with a standard deviation of 15 points.
115 is one standard deviation above average — the mean IQ is defined as 100, not 115.
130 is two standard deviations above average — the threshold commonly used to identify giftedness, not the mean IQ score.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The concept of test validity refers to whether a test:

choices.text: Produces consistent results across repeated administrations
choices.explanation: Consistent results across administrations is reliability, not validity — a test can be reliable but not valid.

choices.text: Measures what it is intended to measure
choices.explanation: Validity is whether a test actually measures what it claims to measure — a test measuring height for an IQ test is reliable but not valid for measuring intelligence.

choices.text: Is fair to all cultural groups equally
choices.explanation: Cultural fairness relates to bias — validity is specifically about whether the test measures the intended construct.

choices.text: Has been standardized on a large sample
choices.explanation: Standardization is a process of establishing norms and procedures — while it can improve validity, validity itself is about whether the test measures what it claims.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following statements about group differences in IQ scores is supported by current scientific consensus?

choices.text: Group differences in IQ scores reflect fixed genetic differences between racial groups
choices.explanation: Current research does not support fixed genetic explanations for group IQ differences — the evidence strongly implicates environmental factors (historical discrimination, test bias, socioeconomic differences, stereotype threat).

choices.text: Group differences in IQ scores are primarily explained by environmental factors
choices.explanation: The scientific consensus is that documented group differences in average IQ scores are primarily attributable to environmental factors — socioeconomic inequality, educational access, stereotype threat, historical discrimination, and test bias — not genetic group differences.

choices.text: IQ scores show no group differences of any kind
choices.explanation: Average score differences between groups have been documented — the question is their cause, which research attributes primarily to environmental factors.

choices.text: Group differences in IQ cannot be reduced through interventions
choices.explanation: Programs addressing early childhood education, nutrition, and socioeconomic disadvantage have shown success in raising scores — demonstrating that environmental interventions can reduce group gaps.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A new intelligence test is administered to the same group of people twice, one month apart. Their scores are highly correlated. This demonstrates which test quality?

choices.text: Content validity
choices.explanation: Content validity involves the test covering all relevant aspects of the construct — high correlation between two administrations demonstrates reliability.

choices.text: Predictive validity
choices.explanation: Predictive validity is how well a test predicts future performance — high correlation across two time points demonstrates test-retest reliability.

choices.text: Test-retest reliability
choices.explanation: Test-retest reliability is demonstrated when the same test produces highly correlated scores when given to the same people on two separate occasions — indicating the test measures something stable.

choices.text: Split-half reliability
choices.explanation: Split-half reliability tests whether two halves of a test are highly correlated — test-retest reliability involves administering the same test twice to the same people.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Crystallized intelligence tends to change with age in which direction?

choices.text: It peaks in early adulthood and then declines sharply
choices.explanation: Sharp early decline describes fluid intelligence — crystallized intelligence follows a very different pattern.

choices.text: It remains stable or increases into old age
choices.explanation: Crystallized intelligence — accumulated vocabulary, knowledge, and skills built from experience — tends to remain stable and may even increase into late adulthood, unlike fluid intelligence which declines.

choices.text: It develops only during childhood and remains fixed thereafter
choices.explanation: Crystallized intelligence continues to grow through adulthood as people acquire more knowledge and experience — it is not fixed after childhood.

choices.text: It is not related to age at all
choices.explanation: Research shows clear age-related patterns for crystallized intelligence — it tends to increase or remain stable into old age, unlike fluid intelligence.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Intellectual disability is defined by which two criteria?

choices.text: IQ below 70 and significant deficits in adaptive functioning
choices.explanation: Intellectual disability requires both an IQ score below approximately 70 AND significant deficits in adaptive functioning (communication, self-care, social skills) — IQ alone is insufficient for diagnosis.

choices.text: IQ below 100 and difficulties in school
choices.explanation: IQ below 100 is below average but not diagnostic — the cutoff is approximately 70, and difficulties in school alone are not sufficient.

choices.text: IQ below 85 and inability to attend regular school
choices.explanation: IQ of 85 is one standard deviation below average — the diagnostic cutoff is approximately 70, and many with mild intellectual disability attend regular schools with support.

choices.text: Genetic disorder and low IQ
choices.explanation: While some intellectual disabilities have genetic causes (like Down syndrome), the diagnostic criteria require IQ below 70 AND adaptive functioning deficits — a genetic cause is not required for diagnosis.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The SAT was originally designed as which type of test?

choices.text: An achievement test measuring high school content mastery
choices.explanation: Achievement tests measure what has been learned — the SAT was originally designed as an aptitude test to predict academic potential.

choices.text: An aptitude test predicting academic performance in college
choices.explanation: The SAT (originally Scholastic Aptitude Test) was designed to measure academic potential and predict college performance — though it has been revised to also assess learned skills.

choices.text: A personality test assessing conscientiousness and work ethic
choices.explanation: The SAT measures cognitive ability — not personality traits like conscientiousness.

choices.text: An IQ test with a standardized scale of 100
choices.explanation: The SAT uses a different scale than IQ tests (originally 200-800 per section) — while related to cognitive ability, it is specifically an aptitude/achievement test, not a traditional IQ measure.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following represents a key ethical concern in psychological testing?

choices.text: Making tests too easy so everyone can pass
choices.explanation: Making tests too easy reduces their ability to differentiate individuals — it is not the primary ethical concern in testing.

choices.text: Using test results without considering factors like stereotype threat and cultural bias
choices.explanation: Ethical concerns in testing include using scores without acknowledging contextual factors that artificially depress performance — stereotype threat, cultural bias, socioeconomic disadvantage can all cause test scores to underrepresent true ability.

choices.text: Providing too much feedback to test-takers
choices.explanation: Providing feedback is generally considered beneficial — the ethical concern is about how test results are used and interpreted in context.

choices.text: Making tests available online where everyone can practice
choices.explanation: Practice effects are a psychometric concern — the primary ethical concern is about fairness, bias, and appropriate use of test results.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Predictive validity refers to:

choices.text: Whether test questions accurately represent the domain being assessed
choices.explanation: Accurate representation of the content domain is content validity — predictive validity concerns future outcomes.

choices.text: How well a test score predicts future behavior or performance
choices.explanation: Predictive validity is determined by correlating test scores with future outcomes — a college entrance exam has good predictive validity if students with higher scores earn higher GPAs.

choices.text: Whether different versions of a test produce the same scores
choices.explanation: Different versions producing the same scores is alternate-form reliability — not predictive validity.

choices.text: Whether the test has been standardized on a large representative sample
choices.explanation: Standardization on a representative sample improves a test's usefulness — but predictive validity specifically concerns how well current scores predict future outcomes.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Why does a high heritability of a trait NOT mean the trait cannot be changed by the environment?

choices.text: Heritability only applies to specific ages, not the entire lifespan
choices.explanation: While heritability can vary across ages, the main reason high heritability doesn't mean immutability is about the population vs. individual distinction.

choices.text: Heritability is a population statistic about variance, not a statement about what environments can produce
choices.explanation: Height has high heritability, yet average heights rose dramatically with improved nutrition — heritability tells us about genetic contributions to within-population variation, not whether environmental interventions can change the population mean.

choices.text: All traits with high heritability are equally changeable by environment
choices.explanation: Traits vary in how responsive they are to environmental change — the key point is that high heritability itself doesn't prevent environmental change.

choices.text: Genes always interact with environment unpredictably
choices.explanation: Gene-environment interactions can be quite systematic — the reason high heritability doesn't preclude change is because heritability is a population variance statistic, not a statement about individual limits.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: David Wechsler's contribution to intelligence testing was to:

choices.text: Develop the first intelligence test for use with children
choices.explanation: Binet created the first practical intelligence test for children — Wechsler later created separate scales that measured intelligence differently.

choices.text: Create intelligence scales that measure verbal and nonverbal abilities separately
choices.explanation: Wechsler developed the WAIS (for adults) and WISC (for children) — scales that provide separate scores for verbal/language abilities and nonverbal/performance abilities, giving a more complete picture of intelligence.

choices.text: Coin the term 'intelligence quotient' (IQ)
choices.explanation: Lewis Terman coined the term 'intelligence quotient' — Wechsler's contribution was developing the separate verbal/performance scoring scales.

choices.text: Identify the general factor 'g' through factor analysis
choices.explanation: The 'g' factor was identified by Spearman through factor analysis — Wechsler's contribution was his intelligence scales emphasizing verbal and nonverbal abilities.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Based on the table, which test type is most appropriate for determining whether a student has mastered the material taught in a history class?

choices.text: Aptitude test
choices.explanation: Aptitude tests predict future performance potential — determining mastery of history content requires measuring what has already been learned.

choices.text: Intelligence test
choices.explanation: Intelligence tests measure general cognitive ability — not specific mastery of history class content.

choices.text: Achievement test
choices.explanation: Achievement tests measure what has been learned in a specific area — a history exam measuring mastery of covered content is an achievement test.

choices.text: Personality inventory
choices.explanation: Personality inventories assess personality traits — not academic content mastery.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Lewis Terman adapted Binet's test into the Stanford-Binet and introduced the concept of IQ as:

choices.text: Mental age divided by the number of years of schooling, multiplied by 100
choices.explanation: Schooling was not used in the formula — Terman defined IQ as mental age divided by chronological age, multiplied by 100.

choices.text: Mental age divided by chronological age, multiplied by 100
choices.explanation: Terman defined IQ (intelligence quotient) as (mental age / chronological age) × 100 — so a 10-year-old with the mental ability of a 10-year-old would have an IQ of 100.

choices.text: Chronological age minus mental age, multiplied by 100
choices.explanation: This formula would produce negative numbers for gifted children — the IQ formula uses mental age divided by chronological age.

choices.text: A deviation score based on how far above the mean one scores
choices.explanation: Deviation IQ (used in modern tests like the WAIS) was developed later — Terman's original IQ formula was mental age divided by chronological age.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Norms in psychological testing refer to:

choices.text: The ethical standards test administrators must follow
choices.explanation: Ethical standards are guidelines for conduct — norms are statistical standards that allow individual scores to be compared to a reference group.

choices.text: Standards for comparing individual scores to a representative sample's performance
choices.explanation: Norms are derived by administering a test to a large, representative sample — they allow individuals' scores to be interpreted by comparison to how similar people scored (e.g., same age group).

choices.text: The expected behaviors of test-takers during administration
choices.explanation: Behavioral expectations during testing relate to standardization of conditions — norms are statistical benchmarks for score interpretation.

choices.text: The correct answers established by expert panels
choices.explanation: Correct answers are established through item analysis, not norming — norms are comparison standards derived from representative samples.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher gives Black and White college students a difficult verbal test. In one condition, the test is described as a measure of intelligence. In another condition, the test is described as a problem-solving task that is not diagnostic of ability. Black students score significantly lower than White students in the 'intelligence test' condition but not in the 'problem-solving task' condition.

questions.question: The results of this study are most consistent with which phenomenon?

choices.text: The Flynn effect
choices.explanation: The Flynn effect refers to rising IQ scores across generations — this study tests a situational manipulation, not generational trends.

choices.text: Stereotype threat
choices.explanation: The study design — where Black students score lower only when framed as an intelligence test — is the classic stereotype threat paradigm from Steele and Aronson (1995). The diagnostic framing activates fear of confirming a negative stereotype, impairing performance.

choices.text: Test bias due to biased question content
choices.explanation: The test content is the same in both conditions — the only difference is the framing, ruling out question-content bias as the explanation.

choices.text: Regression to the mean
choices.explanation: Regression to the mean occurs when extreme scores move toward the average on retesting — this study examines the effect of framing on performance, not extreme score regression.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which term refers to the degree to which a test actually covers all the important aspects of the domain it claims to assess?

choices.text: Predictive validity
choices.explanation: Predictive validity concerns how well test scores predict future outcomes — coverage of the domain is content validity.

choices.text: Construct validity
choices.explanation: Construct validity is the broadest form of validity — whether the test actually measures the psychological construct it claims to. Content validity is specifically about whether items represent the full domain.

choices.text: Content validity
choices.explanation: Content validity is whether a test adequately samples the full domain it claims to measure — a history test with only questions about ancient Rome would lack content validity for a world history course.

choices.text: Test-retest reliability
choices.explanation: Test-retest reliability is about consistency across administrations — content validity is about representativeness of the domain.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following intelligence theories proposes that intelligence consists of a core 'g' factor plus specific 's' factors?

choices.text: Gardner's multiple intelligences theory
choices.explanation: Gardner proposed multiple separate intelligences without a general 'g' — the two-factor theory with g and s factors was proposed by Spearman.

choices.text: Spearman's two-factor theory
choices.explanation: Spearman proposed that intelligence consists of a general factor (g) underlying all cognitive performance plus specific factors (s) unique to particular tasks — derived from his factor analysis of test score correlations.

choices.text: Sternberg's triarchic theory
choices.explanation: Sternberg proposed three types of intelligence (analytical, creative, practical) — not a g+s factor structure.

choices.text: Thurstone's primary mental abilities theory
choices.explanation: Thurstone proposed seven primary mental abilities without a general 'g' factor — this describes Spearman's two-factor theory.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A student scored 130 on a standard IQ test with a mean of 100 and a standard deviation of 15. How many standard deviations above the mean is the student's score?

choices.text: 1 standard deviation above the mean
choices.explanation: One standard deviation above the mean would be a score of 115 — 130 is further above the mean.

choices.text: 2 standard deviations above the mean
choices.explanation: 130 - 100 = 30 points above the mean; 30 ÷ 15 = 2 standard deviations. A score of 130 is 2 SDs above the mean, placing the student above approximately 97.7% of the population.

choices.text: 3 standard deviations above the mean
choices.explanation: Three standard deviations above the mean would be 100 + (3 × 15) = 145 — not 130.

choices.text: 1.5 standard deviations above the mean
choices.explanation: 1.5 SDs above the mean would be 100 + (1.5 × 15) = 122.5 — not 130.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Evidence that intelligence is influenced by genes comes primarily from which research design?

choices.text: Cross-sectional studies comparing people of different ages
choices.explanation: Cross-sectional studies compare age groups — they are used to study development, not to separate genetic from environmental influences on intelligence.

choices.text: Twin studies comparing identical and fraternal twins
choices.explanation: Twin studies compare identical twins (sharing ~100% of genes) to fraternal twins (sharing ~50%) on intelligence measures. Greater similarity between identical twins — especially when raised apart — provides evidence for genetic influence.

choices.text: Case studies of individuals with exceptional intelligence
choices.explanation: Case studies are valuable for in-depth analysis but cannot separate genetic from environmental influences systematically.

choices.text: Longitudinal studies tracking individuals from birth to adulthood
choices.explanation: Longitudinal studies track development over time but do not systematically isolate genetic contributions without comparison groups.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The fact that IQ scores of identical twins reared apart are more similar than IQ scores of fraternal twins reared together supports which conclusion?

choices.text: Environment has no influence on intelligence
choices.explanation: Even identical twins reared apart differ in IQ — demonstrating environmental influence. The finding supports genetic contribution but does not eliminate environmental influence.

choices.text: Genetics contributes substantially to intelligence
choices.explanation: Identical twins (100% shared genes) reared apart are more similar in IQ than fraternal twins (50% shared genes) living together — this demonstrates that genetics contributes substantially more to IQ similarity than shared family environment alone.

choices.text: Intelligence is entirely determined by genetics
choices.explanation: Even genetically identical individuals reared apart differ in IQ — so environment still matters. Genetics contributes substantially but not totally.

choices.text: Shared family environment is the dominant influence on intelligence
choices.explanation: If shared family environment dominated, fraternal twins (same family) would be more similar than identical twins reared apart — the opposite of what's observed.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a projective test used to assess personality?

choices.text: MMPI-2 (Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory)
choices.explanation: The MMPI is an objective self-report inventory with forced-choice items — projective tests use ambiguous stimuli to reveal unconscious processes.

choices.text: Rorschach inkblot test
choices.explanation: The Rorschach is a projective test — it presents ambiguous inkblots and asks people to describe what they see, with responses interpreted as projections of unconscious thoughts, feelings, and needs.

choices.text: Big Five personality inventory
choices.explanation: The Big Five inventory uses explicit self-report questions — not ambiguous stimuli for projection.

choices.text: Wechsler Intelligence Scale
choices.explanation: The WAIS is an intelligence test, not a personality assessment — and it uses structured tasks, not ambiguous projective stimuli.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which criticism of projective tests like the Rorschach is most supported by research?

choices.text: They are too time-consuming to administer
choices.explanation: Time is a practical concern but not the primary research-based criticism — the main concerns are reliability and validity.

choices.text: They lack sufficient reliability and validity compared to objective measures
choices.explanation: Research has consistently found that projective tests like the Rorschach show lower reliability (different interpreters may score differently) and weaker validity (predicting behavior) compared to objective measures like the MMPI — they are criticized as less scientifically rigorous.

choices.text: They reveal too much personal information about the test-taker
choices.explanation: Privacy is an ethical concern but not the primary scientific criticism — the concern is that projective tests may reveal very little reliably valid information, not too much.

choices.text: They can only be used with children, not adults
choices.explanation: Projective tests are used with both adults and children — the main criticism is their questionable reliability and validity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A gifted education program requires an IQ score of 130 or above for admission. This represents which use of a test?

choices.text: Diagnosis of intellectual disability
choices.explanation: Diagnosis of intellectual disability uses IQ cutoffs below 70 — an IQ of 130 is far above average, not below.

choices.text: Identification of giftedness for program placement
choices.explanation: Using an IQ cutoff of 130 (two standard deviations above the mean) to identify students for gifted programs is a typical application of intelligence testing for educational placement.

choices.text: Measurement of crystallized intelligence only
choices.explanation: IQ tests like the WAIS measure multiple components of intelligence — this use is about program placement, not about which component is measured.

choices.text: Assessment of test-retest reliability
choices.explanation: Reliability assessment involves administering the test twice to the same people — using a score cutoff for program placement is a practical application of the test.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The eugenics movement misused intelligence testing by using test results to justify:

choices.text: Providing additional educational resources to low-scoring students
choices.explanation: This would be an appropriate educational use of testing — the eugenics movement used test results in a far more harmful way.

choices.text: Restricting immigration and forced sterilization of low scorers
choices.explanation: The eugenics movement in the early 20th century misused early intelligence test data to argue for restricting immigration of 'inferior' groups and forced sterilization of low scorers — a deeply unethical misapplication of testing.

choices.text: Creating more diverse school curricula
choices.explanation: Curriculum diversification is a positive educational goal — eugenics used testing to justify restricting or eliminating people deemed 'inferior.'

choices.text: Offering scholarships to high-scoring students
choices.explanation: Rewarding high scorers is different from restricting or harming low scorers — the eugenics movement used tests to justify harmful discrimination against those with low scores.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Researchers studied identical twins raised together versus identical twins raised apart. They found that twins raised apart had IQ correlations of approximately .75, while unrelated people raised together had IQ correlations of approximately .30.

questions.question: What conclusion is most directly supported by these findings?

choices.text: Shared family environment is the primary determinant of IQ similarity
choices.explanation: If shared environment dominated, unrelated people raised together would be nearly as similar as identical twins raised apart — but they are much less similar (.30 vs .75), supporting genetic influence.

choices.text: Genetic similarity is a stronger predictor of IQ similarity than shared environment
choices.explanation: Identical twins sharing all genes but raised in different environments still correlate highly (.75), while unrelated people sharing all environments correlate only moderately (.30) — strongly implicating genetics over shared environment in IQ similarity.

choices.text: Environment has no effect on IQ scores
choices.explanation: The .25 gap between identical twins raised apart (vs perfect 1.0 correlation) shows that environment does affect IQ — genes are not the only factor.

choices.text: IQ scores cannot be studied using correlation
choices.explanation: This study uses correlation appropriately to measure similarity — the conclusions about genetic vs environmental influence are directly supported by the correlation data.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Savant syndrome — where someone with a developmental disability has extraordinary specific abilities — is most consistent with which theory of intelligence?

choices.text: Spearman's g factor theory
choices.explanation: If intelligence were a single g factor, savants could not have extremely low overall intelligence alongside one extraordinary specific ability — savant syndrome challenges the notion of a single g.

choices.text: Gardner's multiple intelligences theory
choices.explanation: Savant syndrome — extraordinary specific abilities (musical, mathematical, artistic) despite intellectual disability — supports Gardner's view that different intelligences are relatively independent of each other.

choices.text: Sternberg's practical intelligence theory
choices.explanation: Practical intelligence concerns real-world problem-solving — savant abilities are typically narrow and technical, not broad practical skills.

choices.text: Cattell's fluid intelligence theory
choices.explanation: Fluid intelligence concerns novel problem-solving — savant abilities are often highly specific learned skills, not general reasoning capacity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A researcher finds that a new reading test scores correlate highly with the Stanford-Binet IQ test. This would establish which type of validity?

choices.text: Content validity
choices.explanation: Content validity is about whether items cover the domain — correlating with another established measure is not content validity.

choices.text: Construct validity (concurrent validity)
choices.explanation: Concurrent validity is a type of construct validity — established when a new test correlates with an established measure of the same construct administered at the same time. High correlation with the Stanford-Binet establishes that the reading test measures something related to the intelligence construct.

choices.text: Predictive validity
choices.explanation: Predictive validity involves correlating current test scores with future outcomes — correlating two tests given at the same time is concurrent validity.

choices.text: Test-retest reliability
choices.explanation: Test-retest reliability involves correlating the same test given twice to the same people — concurrent validity involves correlating two different tests.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The TAT (Thematic Apperception Test) asks subjects to create stories about ambiguous pictures. This is an example of:

choices.text: An objective personality test
choices.explanation: Objective tests use specific, structured items with scoring criteria — the TAT uses ambiguous stimuli interpreted by subjects, making it projective.

choices.text: A projective personality test
choices.explanation: The TAT is a projective test — subjects project their own motives, fears, and feelings onto ambiguous pictures when creating stories, revealing unconscious personality dynamics.

choices.text: An aptitude test predicting achievement
choices.explanation: The TAT assesses personality, not cognitive aptitude — it measures psychological themes in storytelling, not cognitive abilities.

choices.text: A standardized achievement test
choices.explanation: Achievement tests measure learned content in specific domains — the TAT measures personality through projective storytelling.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The original formula for calculating IQ was mental age divided by chronological age, times 100. For a 10-year-old child with a mental age of 12, what would the IQ be?

choices.explanation: An IQ of 80 would mean the mental age is below chronological age — this child's mental age (12) exceeds chronological age (10).
An IQ of 100 means mental age equals chronological age — this child's mental age is 2 years ahead of chronological age.
IQ = (mental age / chronological age) × 100 = (12/10) × 100 = 120. A 10-year-old reasoning like a 12-year-old has an IQ of 120 using the original formula.
An IQ of 200 would require a mental age twice the chronological age — (12/10) × 100 = 120, not 200.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which statement about emotional intelligence (EQ) as proposed by Salovey and Mayer is accurate?

choices.text: EQ is the same as IQ measured with different questions
choices.explanation: EQ and IQ are distinct constructs — EQ specifically involves perceiving, using, understanding, and managing emotions, which differs from traditional cognitive intelligence measures.

choices.text: EQ involves the ability to perceive, understand, and manage emotions effectively
choices.explanation: Salovey and Mayer proposed that emotional intelligence involves four abilities: perceiving emotions, using emotions to facilitate thought, understanding emotional meanings, and managing emotions — a distinct domain from traditional cognitive intelligence.

choices.text: EQ cannot be reliably measured because emotions are too subjective
choices.explanation: While EQ measurement is more complex than IQ, researchers have developed ability-based EQ tests — the construct has measurable components despite emotional subjectivity.

choices.text: EQ predicts academic performance better than IQ does
choices.explanation: IQ remains the stronger predictor of academic performance — EQ predicts social and emotional outcomes better than academic achievement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which finding from adoption studies provides the strongest evidence for environmental influence on intelligence?

choices.text: Children's IQ scores resemble their biological parents more than their adoptive parents
choices.explanation: This finding supports genetic influence — evidence for environmental influence would come from showing adoptive environment affects IQ scores.

choices.text: Children adopted into enriched environments show higher IQs than siblings raised in poverty
choices.explanation: When genetically similar children (siblings) are raised in very different environments (one adopted into enrichment, one remaining in poverty), IQ differences emerge — providing direct evidence that environmental quality substantially affects intelligence.

choices.text: Identical twins always have exactly the same IQ regardless of environment
choices.explanation: Identical twins raised apart show IQ differences — if environment had no effect, identical twins would always have identical IQs.

choices.text: Heritability of IQ increases with age
choices.explanation: This finding (actually true) supports genetic influence growing stronger with age — it does not directly demonstrate environmental influence on intelligence.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Reducing stereotype threat in a testing situation can be accomplished by:

choices.text: Making the test longer to reduce scoring variance
choices.explanation: Test length affects reliability — it does not directly address the psychological mechanism of stereotype threat.

choices.text: Telling test-takers that the test has not shown group differences in the past
choices.explanation: Research shows that interventions that remove the diagnostic framing or reframe the situation can reduce stereotype threat — telling test-takers no group differences have been found removes the threat of confirming a stereotype.

choices.text: Increasing test difficulty to discourage surface-level responses
choices.explanation: Increasing difficulty would likely worsen performance for threatened groups — reducing stereotype threat requires removing the threatening framing.

choices.text: Separating test-takers by racial group
choices.explanation: Separation by race makes race more salient, potentially increasing stereotype threat — reducing stereotype threat requires making race less relevant, not more.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A psychologist argues that comparing IQ scores across different cultures is problematic primarily because:

choices.text: IQ tests take too long to administer in some cultures
choices.explanation: Administration time is a logistical concern, not the fundamental problem — cross-cultural comparison raises deeper measurement issues.

choices.text: Tests developed in one culture may measure cultural knowledge rather than the intended construct across cultures
choices.explanation: Tests standardized in Western cultures embed cultural assumptions, vocabulary, and problem formats that may disadvantage non-Western test-takers — making it unclear whether score differences reflect intelligence differences or cultural familiarity differences.

choices.text: Different cultures have genetically different intelligence capacities
choices.explanation: There is no scientific evidence for genetically different intelligence capacities across cultural groups — the concern is measurement validity across cultures.

choices.text: IQ scores are too unstable to compare across any groups
choices.explanation: IQ scores show reasonable stability within populations — the cross-cultural comparison problem is about what is being measured, not score instability.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale (WAIS) is different from earlier IQ tests because it:

choices.text: Uses only verbal questions to measure linguistic intelligence
choices.explanation: The WAIS includes both verbal and performance (nonverbal) subtests — not just verbal questions.

choices.text: Provides separate scores for verbal comprehension and perceptual reasoning
choices.explanation: The WAIS provides separate index scores (Verbal Comprehension, Perceptual Reasoning, Working Memory, Processing Speed) and a Full Scale IQ — giving a more nuanced profile than a single IQ score.

choices.text: Uses the original mental age formula devised by Binet
choices.explanation: The WAIS uses deviation IQ (standard scores based on how one's scores compare to the same age group) — not the original mental age formula.

choices.text: Can only be administered to individuals under age 16
choices.explanation: The WAIS (Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale) is designed for adults ages 16-90 — the WISC is for children.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why heritability estimates for intelligence increase from childhood to adulthood?

choices.text: Genes become more active as a person matures biologically
choices.explanation: While some genes are developmentally regulated, the increasing heritability of IQ is better explained by gene-environment correlation dynamics.

choices.text: Adults have more freedom to select environments that suit their genetically influenced abilities
choices.explanation: Children are largely in environments chosen by parents — as people age, they gain autonomy to select environments, occupations, and activities that match their genetically influenced inclinations, amplifying genetic effects on measured intelligence.

choices.text: Environmental factors become less variable as people age
choices.explanation: Environmental variability does not necessarily decrease with age — adult environments can be quite diverse. The key is gene-environment correlation through self-selection.

choices.text: IQ tests become more reliable at measuring adult intelligence
choices.explanation: Test reliability is a methodological issue — the change in heritability is a real developmental phenomenon explained by gene-environment correlation, not test reliability.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which perspective most strongly emphasizes that intelligence tests reflect cultural values rather than innate mental ability?

choices.text: The genetic/biological perspective
choices.explanation: The genetic perspective emphasizes innate abilities — not cultural construction of test content.

choices.text: The sociocultural perspective
choices.explanation: The sociocultural perspective argues that intelligence tests reflect the values, knowledge, and problem-solving styles emphasized in the dominant culture — what counts as 'intelligent' behavior varies across cultures and contexts.

choices.text: The psychometric perspective
choices.explanation: The psychometric perspective focuses on measuring and quantifying intelligence — while recognizing bias, it does not primarily argue that tests measure cultural values rather than ability.

choices.text: The neuroscientific perspective
choices.explanation: The neuroscientific perspective examines brain correlates of intelligence — not the cultural construction of intelligence tests.
